subject: [SoLL] Gagement - Bigger Bucks: Business Acumen Simulation Training
body:
*School of Lifelong Learning*

soll@dlsu.edu.p*h* <soll@dlsu.edu.ph>





*The DLSU-School of Lifelong Learning (DLSU-SoLL)*

in partnership with

*Gagement®*

cordially invites you to the



*Bigger Bucks: Business Acumen Simulation Training*

Bigger Bucks is a hands-on training where participants run a virtual
company across three financial years, making real decisions on pricing,
investment, staffing, and operations, and immediately seeing the financial
consequences play out on the board game simulation. This is not just a
finance course. It is a Discovery Learning experience as participants learn
by doing, competing, and having honest business conversations with
colleagues across functions. Concepts land because participants live
them. *This
pilot offer will run from August 5 to 6, 2026. *Watch this 30-second video
<https://www.dlsu.edu.ph/announcement/bigger-bucks-business-acumen-simulation-training/>
 from a previous run of the training to gain insights into the learning
experience and activities participants can expect from the program.

*About Gagement*

Gagement has been building commercial acumen in leaders across the Asia
Pacific for over eight years, working with organisations in manufacturing,
government, professional services, retail, pharmaceutical, finance, and
more. Clients include Unilab and Fonterra Philippines.

Bigger Bucks is delivered by Gagement as an authorised partner of Beyond
Bigger Bucks, whose team has been running business acumen simulations
globally for over 30 years across Asia Pacific and Europe.

*Learning Outcome*

Participants will learn to view the P&L, Balance Sheet, and Cash Flow as a
clear narrative of business performance and confidently interpret and
communicate financial insights. They will recognize how small operational
decisions can impact profitability and make financially informed choices
before issues arise. The program will strengthen cash discipline,
commercial awareness across functions, and the ability to assess costs,
value, and returns of business initiatives. Participants will also be able
to build strong business cases and apply their learning through a
practical, measurable improvement project in their own work area.



*Who Should Join*

Senior leaders, operations, sales, and technical specialists who make
decisions affecting costs, revenue, or customer outcomes — regardless of
whether finance is in their job title. Finance professionals and CPAs are
welcome; in almost every session, they attend and find real value because
this is not an accounting course. It is a learning journey to make sense of
business as a whole.



*Modality: *Face-to-face

*More Information: **Training Overview*
<https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/soll/bigger-bucks.pdf>

*Registration Link: **Bigger Bucks Registration*
<https://forms.gle/iqMN7NXQxupEvGc4A>

*Client Feedback: **Read them here*
<https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/soll/bigger-bucks-participants-feedback.pdf>

subject: [OVPERI] Kanazawa University Student Exchange Program Spring 2027
body:
*Office of
the Vice President for External Relations and Internationalization*

*🖂* ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph




* The Office of the Vice President for External Relations and
Internationalization*



*invites undergraduate and graduate students to apply to*



*Kanazawa University, Japan*

*Student Exchange Program for Spring 2027*



Kanazawa University, one of Japan’s National Universities, can trace its
history back to the opening of a small medical school in 1862. Originally a
regional smallpox vaccination clinic, the school has grown over its
150-plus year history to become a leading centre for higher education.



The university campus lies in green hills to the east of the coastal city
of Kanazawa, built on the shores of the Sea of Japan. The university
hospital has its own campus in the city centre.



Kanazawa University has reorganised its faculties into three colleges with
16 schools in-keeping with its aim of being "a research university
dedicated to education, while opening up its doors to both local and global
society".



Kanazawa is an ancient castle city with a long history of education. The
city is surrounded by the Japanese Alps and experiences heavy snows in
winter perfect for skiing and snowboarding. There are also two nearby
national parks and two rivers flow through the centre of Kanazawa.



Its beautiful location (only two-and-a-half hours from Tokyo by train)
means there has always been a community of artists and craftsmen working in
Kanazawa. Ancient castles and temples blend with the city’s modern
infrastructure to create a charming place to study.



The university has many specialist institutes including the Advanced
Science Research Centre, the University Hospital (which dates from the
beginning of the university in 1862), the Institute for Nature and
Environmental Technology and the Centre for Archaeological Research.
Kanazawa welcomes foreign students and partners with many international
institutions across the world. [source: THE
<https://www.timeshighereducation.com/world-university-rankings/kanazawa-university>
]



Exchange period:* April 2027 - September 2027*



*List of study fields*

School of Humanities

School of Law

School of Economics

School of Teacher Education

School of Regional Development Studies

School of International Studies



Explore the syllabus *here*
<https://eduweb.sta.kanazawa-u.ac.jp/portal/Public/Syllabus/SearchMain.aspx>*:
*


*Credits*

1 credit is equivalent to 1.5-2 ECTS.



*Tuition*

Tuition fees at Kanazawa University are waived under the exchange
agreement. Limited slots available.



*Internal Requirements*

*Nomination:* Students must be nominated by DLSU ℅ OVPERI

*Prepare the documentary requirements below:*

   - Outbound Exchange Program Application Form
   <https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/ovperi/outbound-application-form.pdf>
   - Copy of grades
   - Copy of curriculum audit
   - Copy of the EAF (current term)
   - Recommendation letter
   - Application letter
   - Passport bio-page
   - Latest resume

*Eligibility: *


   - Must have completed at least one (1) academic year in DLSU;
   - Must not be a graduating student;
   - Must be enrolled at the time of the program;
   - Must have a cumulative grade point average of 3.0;
   - Must have no failing grade;
   - Must have not committed any major offense as determined by SDFO;
   - Proficient in English or other language that may be required by the
   host University;
   - Must have good potential to succeed in an international academic
   environment;
   - Financially capable to assume expenses for round-trip transportation
   and other travel related costs, insurance, books and school supplies, and
   other personal and living expenses.

*For interested students, kindly follow the steps below:*



   1. Complete this form: <https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9>
   https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9 not later than *July 13, 2026*.
   Ensure that you use your official DLSU email address when filling out our
   registration form; personal email addresses will not be accepted. Those who
   fail to register by the given deadline will not receive the application
   instructions, and their applications will not be acknowledged.
   2. Wait for the email from ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph with detailed
   instructions on how to apply, together with the program fact sheet.
   3. Please note that the OVPERI Application Deadline is on *July 27,
   2026.*



Thank you.

subject: [OVPERI] IITGN ITEC 14–25 September 2026
body:
*Office of
the Vice President for External Relations and Internationalization*

ovperi@dlsu.edu.ph




* The Office of the Vice President for External Relations and
Internationalization*



*would like to share information about the*



*Indian Institute of Technology Gandhinagar *

*Indian Technical and Economic Cooperation Programme*



The Commission on Higher Education (CHED) has announced the call for
applications for the Faculty Development Program hosted by the Indian
Institute of Technology Gandhinagar (IITGN) under the Indian Technical and
Economic Cooperation (ITEC) Programme. This program is designed for faculty
members of ASEAN-based and Global South universities seeking professional
and academic development.



*Program Details:*

   - *Dates:* 14–25 September 2026
   - *Funding Coverage:* The Government of India provides round-trip
   economy airfare, a gratis visa, and local hospitality and transportation in
   accordance with ITEC guidelines.
   - *Application Deadline:* 14 August 2026
   - *How to Apply:* Applications must be submitted directly through the ITEC
   Portal <https://www.itecgoi.in>.



*Inquiries:*

For further questions, interested applicants may coordinate directly with
Mr. Shiv Lal Meena of the Embassy of India, Manila, via email at
edu.manila@mea.gov.in.



[Reference: CHED Memorandum No. 293 series of 2026
<https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/ovperi/MOC-NO-293-S-2026.pdf>
]


*Disclaimer: Please note that the Office of the Vice President for External
Relations and Internationalization (OVPERI) does not provide funding for
this program. This announcement is for information-sharing purposes only.*



*Thank you.*

subject: What the world cup reveals about learning habits around the globe
body:
MCP interview practice, World Cup data, Stanford podcast, Claude insights, and three free sessions.  

AI agent courses, World Cup data, Stanford podcast, Claude insights, and three free sessions.

The Output by DataCamp ( https://www.datacamp.com?utm_source=customerio&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=260707_1-2026-world-cup-insight~n/a~engagement_2-mix_6-tier-2_8-emal-ci_11-weekly-news&dc_euid=20532465 )

A weekly dispatch for AI + Data Practitioners  ·  #003

Every country stopped learning during their World Cup match. Except one.

 

We tracked study time across 20M+ DataCamp learners during the 2026 World Cup. 19 of 20 countries dropped during their match window. Morocco hit −73%. Argentina −58%. Brazil −56%. Belgium went up 53%. Turns out a batch of University of Antwerp students had a DataCamp assignment due that day… Context changes everything.

 

Every country stopped learning during their World Cup match. Except one. ( https://www.datacamp.com/code-along?utm_source=customerio&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=260707_1-2026-world-cup-insight~n/a~engagement_2-mix_6-tier-2_8-emal-ci_11-weekly-news&dc_euid=20532465 )

 

That's the value of looking behind the number, the next skill is knowing what to do with it. The World Cup generates some of the most scrutinized sports data on the planet, and it's all available to practice on. Calculate player performance from FIFA data in How to Deal with Messi Data ( https://www.datacamp.com/code-along/how-to-deal-with-messi-data?utm_source=customerio&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=260707_1-2026-world-cup-insight~n/a~engagement_2-mix_6-tier-2_8-emal-ci_11-weekly-news&dc_euid=20532465 ), or build your own Soccer AI Agent ( https://www.datacamp.com/code-along/build-a-soccer-agent?utm_source=customerio&utm_medium=email&utm_campaign=260707_1-2026-world-cup-insight~n/a~engagement_2-mix_6-tier-2_8-emal-ci_11-weekly-news&dc_euid=20532465 ).

19 countries cheered. Only one kept focus. If you know someone who owes DataCamp some study time this week, now's the moment.

subject: [GCOE] Hybrid Dissertation Final Oral Defense of Engr. Mary Joanne C. Aniñon (Ph.D. CE Candidate)
body:
*Gokongwei College of Engineering*

(632) 524-4611 Ext. 217
january.bencan@dlsu.edu.ph




Department of Civil Engineering

Gokongwei College of Engineering



cordially invites you to the



Hybrid Dissertation Final Oral Defense entitled



*“*Experimental characterization of composite bamboo shear wall panels

under monotonic and cyclic loading*”*



by



*Engr. Mary Joanne C. Aniñon *

*(Ph.D. CE Candidate)*



on



Wednesday, 22 July 2026

Velasco Hall Room 306 (V306)

9:00 - 12:00 noon



Join Zoom Meeting



https://zoom.us/j/92759787683?pwd=A6ke0PDxmJON1IF7AwcjjjmaRbUoe0.1



Meeting ID: 927 5978 7683

Passcode: 248640



Dr. Lessandro Estelito O. Garciano *(Adviser)*

Dr. Richard M. De Jesus *(Panel Chair)*

Dr. Andres Winston C. Oreta *(Panel Member)*

 Dr. Bernard A. Lejano *(Panel Member)*

 Dr. Rodolfo P. Mendoza, Jr. *(Panel Member)*

Dr. Pher Errol B. Quinay *(External Panel Member)*



*Abstract*



Composite bamboo shear wall (CBSW) systems have emerged as a promising
structural alternative for low- to mid-rise buildings because of their
lightweight, renewable, and potentially resilient characteristics. However,
their structural behavior, damage progression, performance-based response,
and practical implementation remain insufficiently understood. This study
evaluated the shear performance, damage behavior, performance-based
characteristics, and implementation potential of CBSW panels through
experimental investigation. Two wall configurations were subjected to
monotonic and cyclic lateral loading following ISO 21581:2010(E) Method I
using a developed hold-down connection detail to restrain uplift and
overturning and isolate in-plane shear behavior. The results demonstrated
that both wall configurations exhibited stable lateral load-resisting
behavior suitable for structural applications. The braced configuration
provided significantly greater lateral strength and stiffness, whereas the
unbraced configuration exhibited higher ductility and deformation capacity,
indicating that wall selection should be based on the desired structural
performance objective. The structural response of CBSW panels was
governed primarily
by the cladding-to-frame connections. The developed fragility curves and
drift-based performance limits provide a practical framework for
performance-based evaluation beyond conventional strength design.
Furthermore, CBSW panels demonstrated the potential to reduce construction
duration and achieve cost competitiveness relative to conventional wall
systems, although bamboo supplier accessibility significantly affected
project lead time and transportation-related costs. Overall, this study
advances the understanding of CBSW systems, providing reference data to
support future design guideline development and the broader adoption of
bamboo-based structural systems.

subject: [DIPO] Advisory on DIPO Review Process for Film Showing Activities
body:
*DLSU Intellectual Property Office (DIPO)*

dipo@dlsu.edu.ph <ndipo@dlsu.edu.ph>


Good day,

The De La Salle University Intellectual Property Office would like to
inform all offices, units, departments, colleges, and recognized student
organizations of the process for activities involving film showing,
screening, or exhibition of audiovisual works.

For film showing activities, DIPO does not issue the final clearance or
approval for the activity. However, since these activities may involve the
public screening of copyrighted audiovisual works, use of film-related
materials, and possible use of DLSU-associated marks in publicity
materials, the contract or agreement must be reviewed from an intellectual
property perspective before submission to the Office of the University
Legal Counsel.

Please be guided by the following process:

*1. Submit the draft contract or MOA to DIPO for IP review*

Before submitting the contract or MOA to OULC, the requesting office or
organization must first send the draft agreement to DIPO for review of
IP-related provisions.

Please submit the draft contract or MOA to:

*Ms. Pamela Raye M. Tadeo*
University Intellectual Property Officer
Email: *pamela.tadeo@dlsu.edu.ph* <pamela.tadeo@dlsu.edu.ph>

Please use the email subject line: *Request for IP Review - Film Showing*

In the body of the email, please include the following details:

   1. Name of requesting office, unit, or student organization
   2. Name and email address of the contact person
   3. Title of the film showing activity
   4. Title of the film or audiovisual work
   5. Name of film provider, distributor, screening partner, or rights
   holder
   6. Target date of the activity
   7. Proposed venue
   8. Whether the activity is ticketed or non-ticketed
   9. Target audience

Please attach the following to the email:

   1. Draft contract, MOA, or agreement
   2. Annexes or attachments to the agreement
   3. Any document or provision showing that the partner is authorized to
   provide the film for the proposed screening, if this is not yet clear in
   the agreement (if available).

*2. Wait for DIPO’s comments*

DIPO will review the draft agreement only from an IP-related perspective.
This may include provisions on screening rights, use of the film title,
posters, stills, trailers, cast names or photos, recording restrictions,
warranties, indemnity, and use of DLSU-associated marks.

DIPO will send its comments or recommendations to the requesting office or
organization.

*3. Review and incorporate DIPO’s comments*

The requesting office or organization should review DIPO’s comments and
incorporate them in the draft agreement, as appropriate.

The requesting office or organization should also ensure that the revisions
are consistent with its arrangement with the external partner.

*4. Submit the revised agreement to OULC*

After incorporating DIPO’s comments, the revised agreement may be submitted
to OULC for legal review and clearance.

Please attach DIPO’s review form when submitting the agreement to OULC.

*5. Submit public-facing materials through DIPO-06 after OULC clearance and
signing*

Once the agreement has been cleared by OULC and signed by the parties, all
public-facing collaterals related to the activity that bear or refer to
DLSU-associated marks must be submitted through *DIPO-06* for review by
DIPO and STRATCOM.

DLSU-associated marks include the University name, seal, logos, “DLSU,” “La
Salle,” “Lasallian,” “Animo,” college or office marks, student organization
names and logos, and other identifiers associated with DLSU.

Public-facing materials may include, among others:

   1. Posters and publication materials
   2. Social media captions and announcements
   3. Registration forms and event pages
   4. Tickets or e-tickets
   5. Certificates
   6. AVP slides, holding screens, or LED layouts
   7. Materials using the film title, poster, stills, cast names or photos,
   partner logo, or other film-related materials

*For every DIPO-06 submission* related to the film showing activity, please
attach the following as reference:

   1. Signed contract, MOA, or agreement
   2. Screenshot or proof of OULC clearance

These attachments are required so DIPO and STRATCOM can confirm that the
submitted collaterals are connected to an activity that has already
undergone the proper contract review and approval process.

For questions or clarifications, you may contact DIPO at *dipo@dlsu.edu.ph*
<dipo@dlsu.edu.ph> or Ms. Pamela Raye M. Tadeo at *pamela.tadeo@dlsu.edu.ph*
<pamela.tadeo@dlsu.edu.ph>.

Thank you.

subject: [DFM] Online Project Paper Defense of Ms. Jiezel Anne P. Sescar
body:
*Department of Financial Management*

ray.almonares@dlsu.edu.ph



The *Department of Financial Management*
*Ramon V. Del Rosario College of Business*


cordially invites you to the Online Project Paper Defense entitled:


*“Greenium and Its Determinants in ASEAN-6 Corporate Bond Markets:*

*Evidence from Green and Conventional Bond Issuances, 2014-2024”*


by

*Jiezel Anne P. Sescar*
*Master of Science in Computational Finance*



*Date: July 11, 2026  Time: 6:30 PM – 8:00 PM*

Zoom Link:
*https://zoom.us/j/96208003669?pwd=ciPpPMz8AanvLDkgY9OBZYAuRXfGhS.1*
<https://zoom.us/j/96208003669?pwd=ciPpPMz8AanvLDkgY9OBZYAuRXfGhS.1>

Meeting ID: 962 0800 3669
Passcode: 665858


*Adviser:*
Dr. Ahmed Meandahawi


*Panel Chair:*
Dr. Junette Perez


*Panel Members:*
Ms. Jan Marie Claire Edra
Mr. John Paul Tanyag




* Abstract *

This study assesses the presence and determinants of greenium in ASEAN-6
corporate green bond markets from 2014 to 2024. Motivated by mixed evidence
on whether green bonds provide lower borrowing costs in emerging markets,
the study examines whether ASEAN-6 corporate green bonds trade at lower
yield spreads than comparable conventional bonds and whether issuer-level,
bond-level, and external factors explain variation in greenium. The
analysis uses a quantitative econometric design based on 5,405 corporate
bond issuances by 506 firms across Indonesia, Malaysia, the Philippines,
Singapore, Thailand, and Vietnam. To improve comparability and reduce
selection bias, propensity score matching with a 1:5 nearest-neighbor
structure is applied, followed by fixed-effects regression models and
robustness checks. The findings indicate a statistically significant
regional greenium, with the preferred matched fixed-effects estimate
showing that green bonds are associated with yield spreads approximately
24.9 basis points lower than comparable conventional bonds. However,
country-level results are heterogeneous and specification-sensitive,
suggesting that greenium in ASEAN-6 remains uneven, fragile, and not
uniformly present across individual markets. Traditional bond fundamentals,
particularly issuer size, maturity, and credit rating, remain the dominant
determinants of yield spreads, while ESG scores and external sentiment
variables show limited and inconsistent pricing effects. Disaster
occurrence is significant only in Thailand, indicating that physical
climate-risk pricing may operate selectively rather than regionally. The
study contributes evidence on sustainable finance pricing in emerging ASEAN
markets and highlights the need for stronger disclosure, market depth, and
regulatory harmonization to support credible green bond valuation.

subject: [HISTORY] Invitation for Dery | Foronda | Ileto Lecture Series - July 15
body:
*Department of History*

( (632) 524-4611 Ext. 572
*** albert.antonano@dlsu.edu.ph




The De La Salle University Department of History, SEARCH (Southeast Asia
Research Center and Hub), and SOCIEDAD DE HISTORIA

Present

*The* Annual History Lecture
*Series*
15 JULY 2026

9:00am–5:00pm The Multipurpose Hall
20th Floor, Br. Andrew Gonzalez FSC Hall

*PART** I. DR. REYNALDO C. ILETO PUBLIC LECTURE SERIES ON SOCIAL AND
CULTURAL HISTORY*
(9:00am-11:30am)

*Sipat sa Historiograpiyang Pilipino: Transkulturalidad at Makakaugnay na
Naratibo sa Hamon ng **Dominanteng* *Pangkasaysayang*
*Diskurso*

*Vicente C. Villan, PhD *Propesor ng Kasaysayan
Unibersidad ng Pilipinas Diliman


*Abstrak:* Ang panayam na ito ay naglalayong sumipat sa praktika ng
historiograpiyang Pilipino sa pamamagitan ng paggamit ng transkulturalidad
at magkakaugnay na naratibo (entangled history) bilang metodolohikal na
lente. Sa halip na teleolohikal na pagbasa na nakabatay sa ideya ng
“nakalaan na kalayaan,” at kanonikal na naratibo na nakasentro sa iilang
“opisyal na milestones,” ipinapakita ng pag-aaral na ang kasaysayan ng
Pilipinas ay masalimuot, magkakaugnay, at plural. Ginagamit ang
pangkasaysayan na ahensya bilang pangunahing balangkas upang ipakita na ang
taumbayan, elit, at imperyo ay sabay-sabay na nakikilahok sa pagbubuo ng
kasaysayan, hindi lamang bilang pasibong tagasunod kundi bilang aktibong
tagapaglikha ng kahulugan sa mga kaganapan. Sa pamamagitan ng pagsusuri sa
mga pangkasaysayang kaganapan gaya ng Tagumpay sa Mactan vs Unang Misa,
Sigaw ng Pugad Lawin, June 12, 1898 Independence Declaration vs July 4,
1946, Japanese Atrocities and American Liberation, at EDSA People Power
Revolution of 1986, lumilitaw ang mga pangunahing mensahe: (1) ang
kasaysayan ay hindi tuwid na linya kundi sabayang daloy ng magkakaibang
karanasan, paggunita, at pananangkapan; (2) ang mga simbolo ng kolonyalismo
ay nagiging sandata ng paglaban sa pamamagitan ng transkultural na pagbasa;
at (3) ang mga naratibo ng kalayaan ay nakapulupot sa ugnayan ng masa, elit,
at imperyo. Natuklasan sa kabuuang pag -aaral na ang transkultural at
magkaugnay na salaysay ay nagbubukas ng alternatibong simula, naglalantad
ng pluralidad ng tinig, at nagtataguyod ng mas inklusibong historiograpiya.
Ang ambag ng papel ay nakaugat sa pagbibigay ng bagong praxis na may
implikasyon sa larangan ng kasaysayan, araling panlipunan, kultural na
pag-aaral, at pambansang identidad. Isang panawagang ipinailanlang na dapat
muling isulat ang kasaysayan ng Pilipinas bilang magkaugnay na naratibo,
upang maging mas makabuluhan para sa bayan at sa hinaharap ng pambansang
dangal.


*PART II. DR. MARCELINO A. FORONDA, JR. MEMORIAL LECTURE SERIES ON LOCAL
AND ORAL HISTORY*
(12:30pm-2:45pm)

*The*
* Babaylan and the Historical Origins of Witchcraft in the Philippines
(1577–1754) Danilo M. Gerona, PhD*

Professor Emeritus
Partido State University


*Abstract:* Early Spanish royal officials envisioned diplomacy, if not
military conquest, as the initial means of securing colonial hegemony
through the submission of the indigenous ruling elite. Yet within the pious
ambiance of the post-conquest mission villages, the embers of indigenous
culture continued to smolder through the persistence of clandestine rituals
led by native priests and priestesses referred to in Tagalog as *Catolonan*,
and in Visaya, *Babaylan*, whose myriad functions transcended the confines
of the purely spiritual. While Spanish officials adopted coercive measures
to extirpate the vestiges of precolonial religion, the missionaries imposed
not only canonical and penal sanctions but also constructed subtle
discourses that demonized these native religious practitioners. Emphasizing
their sinister character, early missionary accounts derisively described
their practices in Western terms as *hechicería *or *brujería*, generically
translated into English as witchcraft.

By introducing the concept of witchcraft, a dreaded European folk belief
historically shaped by inquisitorial proceedings, the friars adroitly
constructed a condemnatory ideological edifice that personified evil and
furnished lurid narratives of defiant and violently diabolical power. Upon
this foundation, they advanced the Christian imperative of reconstituting
native society according to colonial and ecclesiastical norms.

Through a critical examination of primary documents from the ecclesiastical
and civil archives in Spain, as well as records from Manila and local
diocesan archives, this paper investigates the Spaniards’ subtle yet
methodical construction of the sinister image of witchcraft and explores its
subversive implications within the broader colonial project.
PART III. DR. LUIS C. DERY MEMORIAL LECTURE SERIES ON ARCHIVAL RESEARCH
(3:00pm-5:00pm)


*Deciphering the Past: Paleography, Archives, and the Craft of Historical
Research **Francis M. Navarro, PhD*

Professor of History and Director of the University Archives
Ateneo de Manila University


*Abstract:* Every historical inquiry begins with a search—a search for
traces of the past preserved in documents, manuscripts, letters, maps,
and records
that have survived the passage of time. Yet these sources do not readily reveal
their stories. They must first be discovered in archives, patiently
deciphered through the skills of paleography, and carefully interpreted
within their historical contexts. The work of the historian, therefore, is
not simply to recount the past but to engage in a process of recovery,
translation, and understanding.



This lecture reflects on the intimate relationship between archives,
paleography, and historical research. Drawing from the speaker’s experiences
as a historian and archivist, it examines how archival documents—often
overlooked, fragmented, or difficult to read—can illuminate forgotten lives,
communities, and events. It also highlights the crucial role of archives as
custodians of collective memory and paleography as the key that unlocks
access to centuries-old records. At a time when information circulates with
unprecedented speed and when misinformation, disinformation, and fabricated
narratives increasingly challenge public understanding, archives remain
among society’s most trusted sources of evidence. By preserving authentic
records and providing verifiable documentation, archives serve as essential
guardians of historical truth and institutional memory. In an age shaped by
digital technologies, artificial intelligence, and instant communication,
the lecture invites us to rediscover the enduring value of archival research
and the patient craft of reading the past through its original sources.
Ultimately, it argues that archives are not silent repositories of old
records but dynamic spaces where evidence, memory, and historical
understanding converge in the continuing pursuit of truth.



Pre-registration link: https://forms.gle/outZKxM9wczgMKhaA.

*Dr. Rhoda B. Leron*

*Dr. Arnel B. Beltran*



Panel Members

*Dr. Aileen H. Orbecido *(Panel Chair)

*Dr. Angelo Earvin S. Choi *(Member)

*Dr. Marwin R. Gallardo *(Member)



*Abstract *

Textile wastewater containing high concentrations of dyes and dissolved
salts remains a major challenge for sustainable water treatment due to the
difficulty of achieving efficient dye–salt separation. Loose nanofiltration
(LNF) membranes have emerged as promising candidates for this application
by selectively retaining dyes while allowing salts to permeate. In this
study, a zwitterionic polyester LNF membrane was fabricated through
interfacial polymerization between zwitterionic dipyridamole (ZDP) and
trimesoyl chloride (TMC) on a polyethersulfone (PES) substrate to improve
membrane permeability, selectivity, and fouling resistance.

Membrane characterization confirmed the successful incorporation of
zwitterionic sulfobetaine groups into the selective layer, resulting in a
thinner membrane (28.10 nm), lower surface roughness (3.0 nm), enhanced
hydrophilicity (60.2° water contact angle), and a more balanced surface
charge than the unmodified membrane. Under optimized interfacial
polymerization conditions (0.30 wt% ZDP, 0.40 wt% TMC, 5 min immersion
time, and 10 min reaction time), the ZDP-TMC/PES membrane achieved a water
permeance of 65 L m⁻² h⁻¹ bar⁻¹ while maintaining 99.45% Congo red
rejection and only 1.98% NaCl rejection, corresponding to a dye/salt
selectivity of 176.27. Compared with the unmodified membrane, the
zwitterionic membrane exhibited higher permeability, improved dye/salt
selectivity, and excellent antifouling performance, achieving a flux
recovery ratio of 99.86% and stable separation performance during 168 h of
continuous filtration.

These findings demonstrate that incorporating zwitterionic dipyridamole
into polyester loose nanofiltration membranes effectively enhances
hydrophilicity, permeability, dye/salt selectivity, and fouling resistance
while maintaining excellent long-term stability. The developed ZDP-TMC/PES
membrane offers a promising approach for efficient dye desalination and
selective dye recovery.

subject: [DAC] MSA Thesis Final Defense - Mr. Dominic Narag
body:
*Department of Accountancy*

accountancy@dlsu.edu.ph


* The Department of Accountancy*
*Ramon V. Del Rosario College of Business*

cordially invites you to the *Online Final Defense* entitled:

*"The Moderating Role of IFRS 9 Adoption, Audit Quality, and Macroeconomy
in the Bank Earnings Management of ASEAN-5 through Loan Loss Provisions"*

by

*Mr. Dominic N. Narag*
*Master of Science in Accountancy*

*Date:* July 17, 2026 (Friday)
*Time:* 6:00 PM – 7:30 PM

*Zoom Meeting*
https://zoom.us/j/91805741626?pwd=HenCNcm8VId2XQahMCbu9EmraiyEn1.1

*Meeting ID:* 918 0574 1626
*Passcode:* 114115

*Adviser:*
*Dr. Arnel Onesimo O. Uy*

*Panel Chair:*
*Dr. Joy S. Rabo*

*Panel Members:*
*Dr. Jonathan Binaluyo*
*Mr. Aristotle Manuel Go*

*Abstract*

This study examines the moderating role of IFRS 9 adoption, audit quality,
and macroeconomic conditions on earnings management practices among banks
in the ASEAN-5 through the use of loan loss provisions. It investigates
whether banks continue to engage in income smoothing despite the
implementation of IFRS 9 and evaluates how audit quality and macroeconomic
variables influence this behavior.

Using panel data from banks across the ASEAN-5, the study provides
empirical evidence that income smoothing remains prevalent under IFRS 9.
The findings further indicate that banks audited by non-Big 4 firms exhibit
lower levels of income smoothing, while macroeconomic variables are
significantly associated with banks' provisioning behavior.

These findings contribute to the ongoing discussion on financial reporting
quality in the banking industry by highlighting the need for greater
transparency in loan loss provision disclosures and further evaluation of
forward-looking provisioning under IFRS 9.

subject: [OVPERI] Ibaraki University Exchange Program for 1st Semester in AY 2027
body:
*Office of
the Vice President for External Relations and Internationalization*

*🖂* ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph




* The Office of the Vice President for External Relations and
Internationalization*



*invites undergraduate and graduate students to apply to*



*Ibaraki University, Japan*

* Exchange Program for 1st Semester of AY2027*



Founded in 1949, the Ibaraki University (IU) was formed as the result of a
merger between Mito High School (Mito Koto-Gakko), Ibaraki Normal School
(Ibaraki Shihan-Gakko), Ibaraki Juvenile Normal School (Ibaraki Seinen
Shihan-Gakko) and Taga Technical Specialists’ College (Tage Kogyo
Senmon-Gakko). It began with three colleges: the college of literature and
science, the college of education and the college of engineering, but has
now developed to comprise five undergraduate colleges and four graduate
schools. Located in Mito, Ibaraki, Kanto, Japan, IU has three campuses:
Mito campus in Bunkyo, where the college of humanities and science, and
education and science is based. The Hitachi Campus, in Hitachi, home to the
college of engineering and the Ami Campus, in Ami, Inashiki, which is home
to the college of agriculture. All are in the Ibaraki prefecture. IU is
also home to the Izura Institute of Arts and Culture, established to
commemorate Tenshin Okakura, the globally renowned founder of modern
Japanese arts. It specialises in research into the humanities and social
sciences and holds a variety of events throughout the year, from tea
gatherings to special guest lectures. Nagayamon (Okakura’s former house)
and Rokkakudo are also located on the premises, both of which are
registered cultural properties. The Ibaraki Prefecture is in the Northern
part of the Kanto region, which is in the central area of the Japanese
archipelago. Mito is the prefectural capital and is 100km from Tokyo’s
centre, as well as the Narita International Airport. [source: THE]



Exchange period:* April 2027 – July 2027*



*Programs:*



*Program I: Ibaraki Japanese Language and Culture Program*

This program aims to provide students with an opportunity to gain
proficiency in the Japanese language and to deepen their understanding of
Japanese culture and society. Students take the Japanese Training Course
and Japan-related classes taught in English. Intermediate–advanced students
can also take college classes taught in Japanese. A wide range of cultural
and educational activities are also provided during the semester.



*Program II: General Exchange Program*

Students are enrolled in the college or graduate school of Ibaraki
University. As almost all undergraduate classes are conducted in Japanese,
a high level of proficiency in Japanese is required *(Students must possess
N2 level or higher based on the Japanese-Language Proficiency Test (JLPT)*.
However, if students wish to pursue graduate-level research, English
instruction is also available under the permission of a prospective advisor.



*Explore the class list **here*
<https://sites.google.com/view/ibaraki-class-list/%E3%83%9B%E3%83%BC%E3%83%A0>*:
*



*Credits*



For most courses, 13 classes, each 105 minutes, is equivalent to 2 credits.
Some courses offer 1 credit for 13 classes, each 105 minutes.



*Estimated cost of living *

Average living expenses for international students at Ibaraki University
range from about 50,000 JPY to 100,000 JPY per month. (The exact amount
varies depending on choice of housing, food costs, etc.)



*Tuition*

Tuition fees at Ibaraki University are waived under the exchange agreement.
Limited slots available.



*Internal Requirements*

*Nomination:* Students must be nominated by DLSU ℅ OVPERI

*Prepare the documentary requirements below:*

   - Outbound Exchange Program Application Form
   <https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/ovperi/outbound-application-form.pdf>
   - Copy of grades
   - Copy of curriculum audit
   - Copy of the EAF (current term)
   - Recommendation letter
   - Application letter
   - Passport bio-page
   - Latest resume

*Eligibility: *


   - Must have completed at least one (1) academic year in DLSU;
   - Must not be a graduating student;
   - Must be enrolled at the time of the program;
   - Must have a cumulative grade point average of 3.0;
   - Must have no failing grade;
   - Must have not committed any major offense as determined by SDFO;
   - Proficient in English or other language that may be required by the
   host University;
   - Must have good potential to succeed in an international academic
   environment;
   - Financially capable to assume expenses for round-trip transportation
   and other travel related costs, insurance, books and school supplies, and
   other personal and living expenses.

*For interested students, kindly follow the steps below:*



   1. Complete this form: <https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9>
   https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9 not later than *August 17, 2026*.
   Ensure that you use your official DLSU email address when filling out our
   registration form; personal email addresses will not be accepted. Those who
   fail to register by the given deadline will not receive the application
   instructions, and their applications will not be acknowledged.
   2. Wait for the email from ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph with detailed
   instructions on how to apply, together with the program fact sheet.
   3. Please note that the OVPERI Application Deadline is on *September 8,
   2026.*



Thank you.

subject: [DCEP] Erratum - Final Dissertation Defense | Mr. Kevin Yau
body:
*Br. Andrew Gonzalez FSC College of Education*

*Department of Counseling and Educational Psychology*
*🖂*  dcep@dlsu.edu.ph




*The Department of Counseling and Educational Psychology*

 *Br. Andrew Gonzalez FSC College of Education (BAGCED)*



invites you to the



*Online Final Defense*



of



*Mr. Kevin Yau*

(Candidate for PhD in Counseling Psychology)



*“Hope among those with trauma exposures:*

*A mixed methods study on posttraumatic growth (PTG)”*



on



*July 10, 2026 (Friday)*

*3:00 – 5:00 PM*



Join Zoom Meeting



https://zoom.us/j/98391980703?pwd=nKCUGqObITAc4LrmflSwc7fW7iv7My.1&jst=2



Meeting ID: 983 9198 0703

Passcode: 135310



*Adviser*

Dr. Niño Jose Mateo



*Panel Chair*

Dr. Estesa Xaris Legaspi



*Panel Members*

Dr. Leo Capeding

Dr. Aime Guarino

Dr. John Addy Garcia

Dr. Jesus Alfonso Datu



 *Abstract*



This research explored posttraumatic growth (PTG) among Filipino college
students exposed to multiple traumatic events. Using a sequential
explanatory mixed-methods design, the research investigated both PTG
outcomes and the psychological processes through which growth unfolds after
trauma, with particular attention to culturally embedded expressions of
meaning making and hope involved in processing trauma. Study 1 examined the
extent to which cognitive processing of trauma and locus of hope predicted
PTG. Results from 1,775 college students aged 18–24 who had experienced two
or more traumatic events showed that positive cognitive restructuring,
acceptance, internal locus of hope (LOH), external-peers LOH, and
external-spiritual LOH had direct positive effects on PTG. Moreover,
external-spiritual LOH negatively moderated the relationship between
positive cognitive restructuring and PTG. Study 2 explored what cognitive
processing strategies were employed and how locus of hope unfolded in the
process of posttraumatic growth. Fourteen participants with the highest PTG
and LOH levels were interviewed. Reflexive thematic analysis revealed
themes describing growth outcomes connected to self, others, and God,
various strategies used to process trauma, as well as the mechanisms
through which LOH contributed to PTG. These findings underscore the
importance of culturally attuned, growth-oriented, and hope-based
perspectives in trauma-informed care for Filipino emerging adults.



Keywords: posttraumatic growth, locus of hope, cognitive processing of
trauma, meaning making, trauma, traumatic events, Filipino college students.

*Global Urban Innovation & Development Symposium*

*Universiti Brunei Darussalam and Chongqing University*

*26-28 August 2026*



The Global Urban Innovation & Development Symposium is a unified,
interdisciplinary event focused on research, policy, engineering,
infrastructure, and urban innovation. We invite academics, students,
practitioners, and policymakers to join us in exploring these critical
fields. Prizes will be awarded for the best student posters. We encourage
you to share this invitation with interested staff and students.



*Theme:* Urban innovation, sustainable development, and practical pathways
for future-ready cities.



Below are the details for submitting abstracts for oral, poster, or ePoster
presentations. Interdisciplinary contributions in fields ranging from
Engineering to Art and Humanities are encouraged.
*Symposium Tracks*

●      *Track 1: Smart & Resilient Cities* (Data platforms, AIoT, digital
twins, urban climate adaptation)

●      *Track 2: Sustainable Infrastructure & Mobility* (Green transport,
transit-oriented development, net-zero buildings)

●      *Track 3: Urban Health & Well‑Being* (Heat mitigation, environmental
health, inclusive design)

●      *Track 4: Circular Economy & Urban Resources* (Waste valorization,
water/energy systems)

●      *Track 5: Urban Governance & Finance* (Policy instruments, PPPs,
impact investing)

●      *Track 6: Geo‑Environment & Materials for Urban Innovation*
(Nature-based solutions, advanced materials, mineral resources)

●      *Track 7: Coastal & Riverine Urban Systems* (Flood risk, sediment
dynamics, nature-based buffers)

●      *Track 8: Startups / Tech Transfer & Living Labs* (Industry demos,
pilots, and applied showcases)
*Registration Fees (Brunei Dollars)*

●      Early Bird (Student): 200

●      Early Bird (Regular): 400

●      Student Registration: 250

●      Regular Registration: 500

●      ePoster Only: 150 (Note: This does not cover on-site participation)



*Registration Notes:*

●      Student registrations require a valid student ID.

●      Each registered participant may submit up to 2 abstracts.

abstracts.

●      *10 July 2026:* Early Bird registration and payment deadline.

●      *10 August 2026:* Abstract submission deadline.

submissions received from 3 August to 10 August).

●      *20 August 2026:* Regular registration and payment deadline.
(Payments received after 10 August will be treated as attendee-only
registration.)



For more information and FAQs, please visit the official website:
https://guides.ubd.edu.bn/faq/

Relations and Internationalization (OVPERI) does not provide funding for

announcement is for information-sharing purposes only.*



*Thank you.*

*Panel Members*

Dr. Leo Capeding

Dr. Aime Guarino

Dr. John Addy Garcia

Dr. Jesus Alfonso Datu



 *Abstract*



This research explored posttraumatic growth (PTG) among Filipino college
students exposed to multiple traumatic events. Using a sequential
explanatory mixed-methods design, the research investigated both PTG
outcomes and the psychological processes through which growth unfolds after
trauma, with particular attention to culturally embedded expressions of
meaning making and hope involved in processing trauma. Study 1 examined the
extent to which cognitive processing of trauma and locus of hope predicted
PTG. Results from 1,775 college students aged 18–24 who had experienced two
or more traumatic events showed that positive cognitive restructuring,
acceptance, internal locus of hope (LOH), external-peers LOH, and
external-spiritual LOH had direct positive effects on PTG. Moreover,
external-spiritual LOH negatively moderated the relationship between
positive cognitive restructuring and PTG. Study 2 explored what cognitive
processing strategies were employed and how locus of hope unfolded in the
process of posttraumatic growth. Fourteen participants with the highest PTG
and LOH levels were interviewed. Reflexive thematic analysis revealed
themes describing growth outcomes connected to self, others, and God,
various strategies used to process trauma, as well as the mechanisms
through which LOH contributed to PTG. These findings underscore the
importance of culturally attuned, growth-oriented, and hope-based
perspectives in trauma-informed care for Filipino emerging adults.



Keywords: posttraumatic growth, locus of hope, cognitive processing of
trauma, meaning making, trauma, traumatic events, Filipino college students.

subject: [PHILOSOPHY] AI Epistemology Lecture Series
body:
*Philosophy Department*

***christine.calub@dlsu.edu.ph




*The Department of Philosophy *



*cordially invites you to theAI Epistemology Lecture Series*

*July 18, 2026 (Saturday)*
*9:30 AM – 11:30 AM*
*Andrew Building, Room 1506*



The Department of Philosophy warmly invites DLSU faculty members and
graduate students to the AI Epistemology Lecture Series featuring two
distinguished scholars from Hong Kong. The seminar will explore
contemporary issues in artificial intelligence, governance, epistemology,
and ethics through two lectures on AI sovereignty and AI alignment.


*About the Talks*

*A Theory of AI Sovereignty*

*Dr. Adrian Kyle Yee*Hong Kong Baptist University

The advent of increasingly advanced AI systems poses challenges to
traditional concepts of political sovereignty. As AI systems become more
autonomous and powerful, governments may find them increasingly difficult
to regulate and control. This talk outlines seven novel challenges that
autonomous AI systems pose to state sovereignty and proposes five pathways
through which governments may preserve meaningful political authority in
the age of AI.


*Manipulation under Successful RLHF for LLMs*

*Dr. Maomei Wang*Lingnan University

Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) is widely used to align
large language models with human preferences. This talk argues that
manipulation can arise even when RLHF functions exactly as intended. By
examining the relationship between evaluator preferences, user interests,
and model behavior, the paper highlights how AI systems may influence users
in ways that undermine autonomy despite appearing successful by
conventional evaluation standards.

Attendance is limited to *DLSU faculty and graduate students.* Registration
is required. To assist with venue and snack arrangements, kindly register
through the form below *on or before July 10, 2026*:
https://forms.gle/5XPUQHepPmm7rfot6.


*About the Speakers*

*Dr. Adrian Kyle Yee* is Assistant Professor in the Department of Religion
and Philosophy at Hong Kong Baptist University and a founding member of the
AI Safety and Governance Council of Hong Kong. His research lies at the
intersection of the philosophy of artificial intelligence, social and
political philosophy, and the philosophy of science. He has published
extensively on AI governance, misinformation, machine learning, information
poverty, and the societal implications of emerging technologies in leading
international journals such as *Philosophy of Science, Synthese, AI &
Society,* and *Minds and Machines*.

*Dr. Maomei Wang* is a Postdoctoral Research Fellow at the Hong Kong
Catastrophic Risk Centre, Lingnan University. Her research focuses on
formal epistemology, decision theory, Bayesian reasoning, and the ethics
and philosophy of AI. She works on questions concerning rational belief,
uncertainty, machine learning, AI alignment, and the societal impacts of
advanced AI systems, bringing together rigorous formal methods and
contemporary AI ethics.

subject: [OVPERI] CommTECH Camp Highlight 2026 Stream 2: Engineering Innovations
body:
*Office of
the Vice President for External Relations and Internationalization*

*🖂* ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph




* The Office of the Vice President for External Relations and
Internationalization*



*invites undergraduate and graduate students to apply to*



*CommTECH Camp Highlight 2026, Stream 2*

* ITS Surabaya - Indonesia*



*CommTECH *Camp is an opportunity for university students, academics, and
professionals from all over the world to learn about community and global
issues through a variety of insightful but fun activities. The program
includes courses, visitation and discussion with government and
non-government agencies, societal engagement, Indonesian language courses,
learning traditional music and dances, visit to main tourist destinations,
and for sure tasting delicious Indonesian food. The aim of this program is
to facilitate an opportunity to better understand ITS’s expertise in
technology implementation within society while visiting, discovering, and
experiencing Indonesia’s diverse heritage and culture. It will also open up
an opportunity to exchange ideas and develop a mutual goal for the future
of our community.

The program's theme is *“Solving Local Problems with Global Knowledge.*”

*Stream 2: Engineering Innovations on  27 August – 7 September 2026*

CommTECH Camp: Innovations in Engineering offers a captivating exploration
into ITS’s vast innovation in the engineering field, aims to educate and
empower young individuals interested in our latest engineering research and
products. Through a series of workshops, seminars, and hands-on activities,
participants learn about the latest technologies, sustainable practices,
and emerging trends in the engineering sector. The program also focuses on
fostering leadership skills, encouraging innovation, and promoting a deeper
understanding of engineering fields.

*Certificate & credit*

Participants in this program can earn a certificate. ITS will prepare a
credit-earning letter (up to 3 credits) for those who would like to request
one.



*Fees*

The fee for this program is *USD 1,600*. The fees cover accommodation,
local transportation, tuition fees, workshop materials, seminar kits,
lunch, trips, airport pick-up & drop-off services, and vouchers. The fee
excludes personal expenses, round-trip flight ticket, visa, & health
insurance.



*Scholarship for the course fee*

Full scholarship, 50% scholarship (pay only USD 800) & partial scholarship
(pay only USD 1,000) for limited participants. Each university has only one
chance to get one slot for a full scholarship, one slot for a 50%
scholarship & one slot for a partial scholarship.



*Internal Requirements *

*Nomination:* Students must be nominated by DLSU ℅ OVPERI


*Prepare the documentary requirements below:*

   - Outbound Exchange Program Application Form
   <https://old.dlsu.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/pdf/ovperi/outbound-application-form.pdf>
   - Copy of grades (from the MLS)
   - Copy of curriculum audit (from the MLS)
   - Copy of the EAF (current term)
   - Endorsement letter from the College Dean and Department Chair
   - Passport bio-page
   - Latest resume

*Eligibility: *


   - Must have completed at least one (1) academic year in DLSU;
   - Must not be a graduating student;
   - Must be enrolled at the time of the program;
   - Must have a cumulative grade point average of 3.0;
   - Must have no failing grade;
   - Must have not committed any major offense as determined by SDFO;
   - Proficient in English or other language that may be required by the
   host University;
   - Must have good potential to succeed in an international academic
   environment;
   - Financially capable to assume expenses for round-trip transportation
   and other travel related costs, insurance, books and school supplies, and
   other personal and living expenses.

*For interested students, kindly follow the steps below:*


   - Complete this form: <https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9>
   https://forms.gle/9BrNW3AANHNzvd1n9 not later than *July 10, 2026*.
   Ensure that you use your official DLSU email address when filling out our
   registration form; personal email addresses will not be accepted. Those who
   fail to register will not receive the application instructions, and your
   application will not be acknowledged.
   - Wait for the email from ovperi.exchange@dlsu.edu.ph with detailed
   instructions on how to apply and the fact sheet.
   - Please note that the OVPERI Application Deadline is on *July 16, 2026.*

Thank you.

subject: [OVPERI] Chung Yuan Christian University ACUCA Student Mobility Scheme (SMS) - Spring 2027
body:
*Office of
the Vice President for External Relations and Internationalization*

* The Office of the Vice President for External Relations and
Internationalization*



*invites undergraduate and graduate students to apply to*



*Chung Yuan Christian University, Taiwan*

* ACUCA Student Mobility Scheme (SMS) - Spring 2027*



Chung Yuan Christian University (CYCU) was initially established as Chung
Yuan Christian College of Science and Engineering in 1955 by a group of
Christian Taiwanese and American educators looking to train emerging
engineering and science talent. The school's founding motto was "education
through honest, diligent pursuit and practical experience", and it was
upgraded to a full university and renamed Chung Yuan Christian University
in 1980. It has seven faculties with a particular focus on science,
engineering, business, design and law. In its mission statement, CYCU
states that it is "founded on the spirit of Christian love for the world.
With faith, hope, and love, we endeavor to promote higher education for the
benefit of the Chinese people, aiming at the pursuit and advancement of
genuine knowledge in order to maintain our cultural heritage and, thus, to
serve humankind". The institution's Office of International and
Cross-Strait Education was set up by the university to engage in a variety
of international affairs. It promotes exchanges of students with sister
universities across the world, and provides services for international
students preparing to study at CYCU. Notable alumni of the private
university include Chang Fan, a former deputy minister of finance in
Taiwan; Hwang Jung-chiou, chairman of Taiwan Power Company and former
vice-minister of the Ministry of Economic Affairs, and Deng Feng-zhou, a
poet, historian, and environmentalist. The university's campus is located
in the Zhongli District of Taoyuan City, Northern Taiwan. It has a humid
subtropical climate, with mild to warm winters and hot summers. [source:
THE]

College of Science

College of Engineering

College of Business

School of Design

College of Humanities and Education

School of Law

College of Electrical Engineering and Computer Science

College of Semiconductor Technology

subject: [12TH] LSSUMMIT 2026 CALL FOR ATTENDEES: ID122 UG ANNOUNCEMENTS
body:
The Lasallian Scholars Society under the Office of Student LIFE calls for attendees for LSSUMMIT 2026

Eager to reach the peak? Pre-registration season is finally here!
LSSUMMIT 2026 is here! Senior high and college students this one’s for you!

Carrying forward the momentum of past iterations, the Lasallian Scholars Summit (LSSUMMIT) this year is dedicated to exploring what the title of 'scholar' means for our community and in uncovering meaningful opportunities to bring that vision to life. We seek to advocate for scholars who can leverage their love for learning in producing knowledge and engaging more actively in nation-building. Our theme, “Reimagining Research as a Field of Encounter and Fraternity”, highlights the potential of Lasallian scholars to lead through research, seeing it as a tool to contribute to the Lasallian Mission’s goal for genuine social development. At the heart of this year’s LSSUMMIT, it is with hope that by hosting a flagship research conference alongside a lineup of talks and activities, research can be made a more accessible concept. We are convinced that this presents as a good and timely opportunity to make a dent in developing a stronger and fraternal research culture in the Philippines.

What you can get from LSSUMMIT 2026: Expect inspiring talks on overcoming research hurdles, fun, interactive activities, and a
chance to witness how research can drive genuine social impact. You’ll get to meet and connect with students from other universities!

Date: July 18, 20-21 2026
Venue: OL, De La Salle University

What to wear: Formal attire

subject: [LIBRARIES] American Corner Manila - Freedom250: Shared Histories of Democracy, Power, and Exchange
body:
*Libraries*

(632) 8524-4611 Ext. 620
library@dlsu.edu.ph






*DLSU Libraries’ American Corner Manila*in partnership with



*DLSU Department of International Studies*cordially invite all DLSU
students, faculty members, and staff to join the learning session titled


*Freedom250: Shared Histories of Democracy, Power, and Exchange 21 July
2026 (Tuesday), 1:00 PM - 2:30 PM*American Corner Manila, The Learning
Commons, 14F Henry Sy Sr. Hall, DLSU Manila Campus

*About the Program*

This session explores the intertwined histories of the United States and
the Philippines as part of the commemoration of the 250th anniversary of
American independence. Moving beyond a singular national narrative, it
examines how democracy, empire, and institutional exchange shaped the
historical relationship between the two countries. Through a comparative
lens that includes the experiences of the Philippines and Puerto Rico under
American colonial rule, the discussion highlights how colonial institutions
were interpreted, adapted, and transformed within local contexts.
Participants will critically reflect on the historical meanings of empire
and the enduring legacies of colonialism in shaping political institutions,
civic life, national identities, and contemporary U.S.-Philippine
relations. The emphasis is on:


   - The contrasting development of democracy in the Philippines and Puerto
   Rico under American colonial rule;
   - How colonial institutions were interpreted and adapted rather than
   simply transplanted;
   - The historical meanings of empire and their influence on political
   institutions and identities; and
   - The enduring legacies of colonialism in shaping contemporary
   Philippine politics and U.S.-Philippine relations


*Resource Speaker: Mr. Angelo Miguel Galang*Lecturer, De La Salle University


*Registration*Participation is free and open to all DLSU community members.
Kindly complete this online registration form
<https://libcal.dlsu.edu.ph/event/5911646> to secure your slot.


*Contact Information*Faculty members interested in incorporating this
program as a fourth-hour activity for their classes, as well as those with
other  inquiries,  may  reach  out  to  Ms.  Roana  Flores-Solemos,
American  Corner  Librarian, at roana.flores@dlsu.edu.ph.

You can also reach AC Manila via landline at (632) 8524 46 11 loc. 265 or
connect with us through our Facebook <https://www.facebook.com/acmanilaph/>
, Instagram
<https://www.instagram.com/acmanilaph?igsh=MWw2ZTVzMHVvNm0zeg%3D%3D&utm_source=qr>,
and Spotify platforms
<https://open.spotify.com/show/64daEgToc72iDzhBEti226?si=b06106e0ca2346f2&nd=1&dlsi=8f197edd09f24436>

subject: [DSE] Online Final Defense of Aubrey D. Macaspac
body:
*Department of Science Education*

emperatriz.murillo@dlsu.edu.ph



*The Department of Science Education*

*Br. Andrew Gonzalez FSC College of Education (BAGCED)*



invites you to the

*Online Final Defense*



of



*AUBREY D. MACASPAC*

Candidate for Doctor of Philosophy in Science Education major in Biology



*"DISKURSO: Dialogic Intervention and Scheme for Knowledge-building Using
Reflective Scientific Oracy”*



on



*JULY 6, 2026*

*10:00 AM – 12:00 NN*



Join Zoom Meeting

https://zoom.us/j/94925598638?pwd=8oAVnVE5Jbl9V1qtHpuoAWb4iFjutO.1



Meeting ID:  949 2559 8638

Passcode:  003732



 *Adviser:*   Maricar S. Prudente, Ph.D.



*Panel Chair: *Voltaire M. Mistades, Ph.D.



*Panel Members*

Lydia S. Roleda, Ph.D
Denis Dyvee R. Errabo, Ph.D.

Mary Jane C. Flores, Ph.D.

Socorro E. Aguja, Ph.D.

(External Examiner)



*ABSTRACT*



Classroom discourse constitutes a foundational mechanism for science
learning, where ideas and reasoning are negotiated through dialogue. This
study introduces DISKURSO: Dialogic Intervention and Scheme for
Knowledge-Building Using Reflective Scientific Oracy, an action research
project that integrates the development of a coding scheme with the
implementation of a dialogic teaching intervention in a bilingual
undergraduate biology classroom. Informed by existing discourse-analytic
frameworks, the coding scheme was conceptualized to capture the
multidimensional nature of discourse in Filipino science classrooms. It
operates across three interrelated analytical dimensions: (1) Dialogic
Moves, (2) Epistemic Language Functions, and (3) Code-switching Patterns.
The study is theoretically grounded in Vygotsky’s sociocultural theory and
further informed by other relevant theories on dialogism. Employing a
mixed-methods, multi-phase (PDSA) action research design, data were
collected through audio-recorded interactions, questionnaires, and
interviews. Students’ perceptions of dialogic practices increased
substantially from pre-implementation (*M* = 8.45) to post-implementation (
*M* = 9.62), as did the teacher’s self-assessment of her own dialogic
practices. Transcript analysis revealed a shift from IRE to a dialogic
pattern of Elicit → Response → Reflective Toss → Build On, with students
demonstrating evidence-based reasoning, peer challenge, and cumulative
talk. Two key implications emerge: first, dialogic patterns transfer
epistemic responsibility from teacher to learners, positioning students as
central contributors to knowledge construction; second, bilingualism
constitutes an asset for science learning. Grounded in the linguistic,
cultural, and epistemic contexts of the Philippines, DISKURSO affirms that
effective science learning emerges from tools that are *of Filipino, by
Filipino, *and *for Filipino.*

*Keywords: *classroom dialogue, scientific discourse, coding scheme,
dialogic teaching, dialogic intervention.

subject: [DTRE] Dissertation Proposal Defense of Mr. John Uppie Adolfo
body:
*Department of Theology and Religious Education*

jeff.corpuz@dlsu.edu.ph




The *Department of Theology and Religious Education*

*College of Liberal Arts*



cordially invites everyone to the

Proposal Defense of the Dissertation entitled



*From Doctrine to Praxis:*

*Religious Education and Ethical Decision-Making among Selected*

*Senior High School Students in the Philippines*



*Mr. John D. Adolfo*

(Candidate, Doctor of Philosophy in Applied Theology with Specialization in
Religious Education Ladderized Program)



on



*July 11, 2026 (Saturday)*

9:00 AM

via ZOOM



*Join Zoom Meeting*

https://zoom.us/j/97261013508?pwd=PghXrOdvRLLr2mf1qAluAKbHEtfpmM.1

Meeting ID: 972 6101 3508

Passcode: DTRE



Mentor: *Prof. Dr. Dalmacito Cordero, Jr.*



Panel Chair: *Sr. Dr. Auria U. Arabit, SdP*



Panel Members

*Prof. Dr. Fides del Castillo*

*Dr. Dorothy Javier-Martinez*

*Prof. Dr. Willard Macaraan*

*Dr. Teresa Camarines*



*Abstract*



Religious Education (RE) is a core component of Catholic education, aiming
to foster ethical formation, moral discernment, and integral human
development. While existing studies have examined doctrinal instruction,
values formation, and pedagogical approaches, relatively little qualitative
research has explored how Filipino senior high school students themselves
experience and interpret the influence of Religious Education on their
ethical decision-making. Addressing this gap, this study investigates how
selected senior high school students describe, interpret, and negotiate the
role of Religious Education in shaping their ethical decision-making.
Guided by an interpretive qualitative design with a phenomenological
orientation, the study will employ semi-structured interviews with twelve
purposively selected students from two Catholic senior high schools in
Metro Manila. Data will be analyzed using Reflexive Thematic Analysis.
James Rest's Four-Component Model will serve as the primary analytical
framework, while the Filipino ethical concepts of kapwa, loób, and
pagpapakatao will provide contextual interpretive lenses. The study aims to
contribute to Applied Theology and Religious Education by offering a
contextually grounded understanding of ethical formation among Filipino
Catholic senior high school students. Its findings may also inform
pedagogical practices and support the development of a contextually
grounded framework for strengthening ethical competence in Philippine
Catholic senior high school Religious Education.



Keywords: adolescents, ethical decision-making; moral formation; Filipino
ethics; phenomenology.

subject: [PSYCHOLOGY] Professorial Chair Lecture
body:
*Department of Psychology*

( (632) 524-4611 Ext. 542
***psychology@dlsu.edu.ph




*The Department of Psychology*

*College of Liberal Arts*



cordially invites you to a lecture for the



*Dr. Pao Shih-Tien Distinguished Professorial Chair in Organizational
Development*



*10 July 2026, Friday, 2:30 – 4:00 p.m.*



*In-Person*

*Y507 Seminar Room, Yuchengco Hall*



A Zoom option is also available

https://zoom.us/j/93734994332?pwd=URMITnjWqPhb7Napnh3OUsWQTs18R5.1

Meeting ID: 937 3499 4332

Passcode: 893548



*Toward a Typology of Minority Exclusion and Work Fulfillment across
Countries*

*Melissa Lopez Reyes*

Professorial Chair Holder

Full Professor of Psychology



*Abstract:*

Minority workers across societies differ not only in the forms and extent
to which they face exclusion in the workplace, but also in the extent to
which they experience work fulfillment.  Using the Work Orientations V
survey of the International Social Survey Programme (28 countries, 51,667
workers), this lecture examines majority-minority gaps in workplace
exclusion (discrimination, harassment, employment insecurity) and work
fulfillment (opportunities, work valuing, commitment, well-being).
Country-level gap indices represented in quadrant plots suggest stable
patterns of minority exclusion vis-à-vis work fulfillment that reflect
inclusive, exclusionary, resilient, and included-but-detached
configurations.  Selected countries that consistently occupy the same
quadrant across plots are profiled to illustrate these configurations. The
lecture advances a typological perspective on minority exclusion and work
fulfillment and offers a heuristic for examining the organizational
conditions under which minority workers are incorporated or remain
resilient in the workplace.



*About the Chair Holder:*

Melissa’s recent research examines social boundaries—the often invisible
lines that separate insiders from outsiders and shape who belongs and has
access to opportunities. Drawing primarily on international large-scale
surveys, Melissa uses the constructs of boundary setting and permeability
to examine citizens’ criteria for national inclusion of migrants, youth
political engagement in periphery and semi-periphery countries, the
geopolitical divide in measuring global competence, and social-mobility
attributions across the social hierarchy.



*About Dr. Pao Shih-Tien:*

Dr. Pao Shih-Tien played a pivotal role in the development of Chiang Kai
Shek College in Manila over more than three decades of educational
leadership. He was appointed Vice-Principal in 1951, Principal in 1959, the
College’s first President in 1965, and Chairman of the Board of Trustees in
1983. Under his leadership, Chiang Kai Shek College became the first
Chinese-Filipino institution to offer education from kindergarten through
college.  During his presidency, the College adopted the prescribed
Philippine curriculum under the Filipinization of Chinese schools while
retaining Chinese Language Arts as an integral component of the curriculum.

subject: [DEAL] Online Final Dissertation Defense of Mr. Ruzheng Pei
body:
*Department of English and Applied Linguistics*

kristine.marie.lim@dlsu.edu.ph




The



*Department of English and Applied Linguistics (DEAL)*



invites you to the



Online Final Dissertation Defense



of




*Mr. Ruzheng Pei*

(Candidate, Doctor of Philosophy in Applied Linguistics)



titled



*English Language Teaching and Emotions: Exploring Chinese Novice
Vocational University English Teachers’ Emotion Labor*


on



*July 8, 2026 (Wednesday)*

*2:00-4:00 pm | via Zoom*



*Join Zoom Meeting*

 https://zoom.us/j/96503227862?pwd=6nYSWxyXZY1lgmlYbpNA6yDGORYD7b.1&jst=2



Meeting ID: 96503227862

Passcode: 184210



*Adviser:*

Rochelle Irene G. Lucas, Ph.D.



*Panel Chair:*

Aireen B. Arnuco, Ph.D.



*Panel Members*:

Aileen B. del Rosario, Ph.D.

Jonna Marie A. Lim, Ph.D.

Ariel Robert C. Ponce, Ph.D.



*External Panel Member:*

Edward Jay M. Quinto, Ph.D.



*Abstract*



Emotion labor has become an important issue in English language teaching
(Hargreaves, 2000; Yin, 2015), yet little is known about how novice English
teachers in Chinese vocational universities experience and manage it. This
study addresses that gap by examining the emotional demands faced by
first-year vocational university English teachers, the factors shaping
those demands, and the strategies teachers use in response to institutional
and national education policies. Using qualitative thematic analysis, data
were collected from six novice English teachers. The findings show that
teachers' emotion labor is shaped by interconnected individual,
institutional, and sociocultural factors. Institutional expectations and
national policy requirements both influence how teachers display, regulate,
and interpret emotions in the classroom, though in different but related
ways. The study argues that emotion labor should be understood
ecologically, through the interaction of teacher experience, school
structures, and policy contexts. It offers implications for future teacher
support, curriculum development, and learning-material design in practice.



Keywords: emotion labor; Chinese novice vocational university English
teachers; qualitative thematic analysis; emotions in ecosystem; education
policy.

subject: [BIOLOGY] Dissertation Proposal Defense of Kristine Grace D. Waing
body:
*Department of Biology*

*🖂* albert.rosana@dlsu.edu.ph



The Department of Biology

College of Science (COS)



cordially invites everyone to the



Proposal Defense of the dissertation entitled

*Optimum Culture Conditions, Mycochemical Composition, and Biological
Activities of Hericium erinaceus (Bull.) Pers. (1797)*



by



*Kristine Grace D. Waing*

(Candidate, Doctor of Philosophy in Biology)



*11 July 2026 at 9:00 -12:00 PM*

https://zoom.us/j/9037099478?pwd=uLzBtPEP9Z7x6KrC9W97NEaFNmQuvQ.1&omn=94986685283

Meeting ID: 903 709 9478
Passcode: KGDW071126


*Adviser*

Dr. Rich Milton R. Dulay



*Panel*

Dr. Mariquit M. De Los Reyes, Chair
Dr. Billy Joel M. Almarinez, Member
Dr. Mark Christian Felipe R. Redillas, Member

Dr. Sofronio P. Kalaw, External Committee Member

Dr. Edwin R. Tadiosa, External Committee Member



*Abstract:*



Mushrooms play a vital role in our ecosystems and are in demand for their
nutritional and therapeutic benefits. Among them, *Hericium erinaceus,
*commonly
known as Lion's Mane, is a temperate mushroom which is highly valued for
its various biological activities, including antioxidant, antimicrobial,
antidiabetic, and anti-inflammatory effects, due to its secondary
metabolites. Nowadays, the global mushroom industry is shifting towards the
use of agro-industrial waste in mushroom production. In the Philippines,
the Center for Tropical Mushroom Research and Development at Central Luzon
State University had successfully implemented zero rice waste technology by
recycling and converting abundant rice straw into organic substrates, which
are being utilized as a growing medium which successfully domesticated and
cultivated several mushroom species. However, there is limited information
on how to successfully cultivate this temperate mushroom under tropical
Philippine conditions. Because a mushroom's growth, yield, and biological
activities depend on its environment and substrate, thus, finding the
optimum cultivation conditions is essential. This study addresses that gap
by exploring how *H. erinaceus* performs using rice hull and rice bran.
Ultimately, optimizing this cultivation process will not only advance
sustainable farming and boost rural livelihoods but also the possibility of
nutraceutical development. Thus, the present study generally aims to
investigate the optimal culture conditions, mycochemical composition, and
biological activities of *H. erinaceus.*



*Keywords:*



*Cultivation; mushrooms; rice hull; zero rice waste technology.*

subject: [LSPO] First Holy Communion 2026
body:
*Lasallian Pastoral Office*

*** lspo@dlsu.edu.ph


*To the Lasallian Community:*


The Lasallian Pastoral Office is pleased to announce the celebration
of the *First
Holy Communion* for members of the DLSU community who have not yet received
this sacred sacrament. In the *Holy Eucharist*, the *source and summit of
the Christian life* (Lumen Gentium, 11), we receive the real presence of
Jesus Christ—Body, Blood, Soul, and Divinity. Through this sacrament, we
are united more deeply with Christ and His Church and are strengthened to
live our faith in our daily lives with greater love and purpose.

We invite those who have already been baptized in the Catholic Church and
wish to receive the Sacrament of the Eucharist for the first time to join
this year’s *First Holy Communion 2026*.

This is open to the following members of the DLSU community:

·  Senior High School
·  Undergraduate Students
·  Graduate Students
·  External Service Personnel
·  Staff and Personnel

*REQUIREMENTS:*

1.     Application Form – *First Holy Communion Registration Form 2026*
<https://docs.google.com/forms/d/e/1FAIpQLSf5j70Q0WQ3WBt-oNSdvl0SY93sTg3znVzjncO623qJHMazFw/viewform?usp=dialog>

2.     Attendance to the Catechism & Seminar

*IMPORTANT DATES TO REMEMBER:*


*July 8, 2026 (Wednesday) *
*Catechism and Seminar *2:00 PM – 4:00 PM
Pearl of the Great Price Chapel


* First Holy Communion Day *Date and Time: To Be Announced
Pearl of the Great Price Chapel

For inquiries and further information, you may email *lspo@dlsu.edu.ph*
<lspo@dlsu.edu.ph> or contact *maria.gina.cejuela@dlsu.edu.ph*
<maria.gina.cejuela@dlsu.edu.ph>

Thank you.

subject: [OUR] Advisory: Beware of suspicious phone calls regarding course enlistment: De La Salle University
body:
There have been reports that some members of the academic community have received suspicious calls regarding course enlistments. This is also experienced by students in other universities and colleges.

Everyone is strongly advised not to entertain suspicious calls or emails, especially those requesting personal or sensitive information or asking for payments. Please note that such activities are not part of the standard procedures followed by De La Salle University personnel and are considered unacceptable practices. 

Only official University channels are used for communications related to enrollment, payments, accounts, verifications, and other concerns. 

Moreover, community members are strongly advised to refrain from posting screenshots of any part of the Archer’s Hub portal online. 

For inquiries and concerns on enlistment/enrollment, payment and assessment, go to [Service Request] (https://archershub.dlsu.edu.ph/) > Raise Ticket at the Archer’s Hub.

For suspected or confirmed data security incidents, please email [databreach@dlsu.edu.ph] (mailto:databreach@dlsu.edu.ph). 

Thank you. 

https://dlsu.instructure.com/






________________________________________

https://dlsu.instructure.com/profile/communication
